title: Non-Clifford Crosstalk Noise in Surface Codes Using Hybrid Stabilizer-Tensor Network Methods
authors: Ben Harper, Azar C. Nakhl, Martin Sevior, Muhammad Usman
abstract: Scalable realisation of quantum computing is reliant on the development of fault tolerant devices. Analysis of quantum error correction protocols typically considers incoherent noise models or noise-free syndrome measurements. While this is simple to simulate classically and straightforward to compute analytically, these simplifications are unable to capture the full dynamics of a noisy quantum system. In this work we use advanced hybrid stabilizer-tensor network simulation techniques to simulate coherent quantum crosstalk noise during syndrome extraction on a surface code. We show that the inclusion of coherence increases logical error rates and lowers the code threshold. In addition, we show that the specific distribution of the noise can quantitatively change logical error rates. The methods in this work allow simulation of quantum error correction with noise models previously inaccessible to classical simulation, providing new insights on the effect of crosstalk noise on quantum error correction codes.
summary: This paper simulates quantum error correction on surface codes using advanced tensor network methods to account for coherent crosstalk noise. It demonstrates that this coherence significantly degrades performance, increasing logical error rates and lowering the error threshold compared to simpler noise assumptions. The work provides a vital tool for accurately characterizing hardware limitations in scalable quantum computing.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of simulating quantum error correction (QEC) under realistic, coherent quantum crosstalk noise, which is beyond the scope of simpler noise models or classical simulations.
main_result: The inclusion of coherent crosstalk noise significantly increases logical error rates and lowers the code threshold compared to idealized models, demonstrating that standard approximations fail to capture the full dynamics.
method: The authors employ advanced hybrid stabilizer-tensor network simulation techniques (using GCAMPS) to simulate syndrome extraction on surface codes, allowing for the inclusion of coherent noise terms.
model_or_system: The physical system is the surface code, a topological stabilizer code used for fault-tolerant quantum computation. The noise model specifically incorporates gate-based nearest neighbor crosstalk noise modeled as a coherent ZZ rotation.
key_observables: Logical Error Rate ($P_L$) and the Code Threshold.
important_parameters: Crosstalk parameter ($	heta$), physical error rates ($p_1, p_2$), and code distance ($d$).
main_assumptions: The simulation results are a lower bound due to tensor network truncation; previous studies often relied on stochastic approximations of coherent effects.
figures_summary: Figures compare logical error rates for various noise models (Pauli vs. coherent crosstalk vs. random sign noise) across different code distances, showing the quantitative impact of coherence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of coherent noise in QEC, details the hybrid simulation method, presents results comparing coherent noise to idealized models, and concludes by emphasizing the necessity of accurate noise characterization.
why_interesting: The work provides a rigorous, scalable simulation framework for incorporating non-Markovian and coherent noise sources into QEC, which is crucial for designing fault-tolerant quantum hardware based on superconducting qubits or similar platforms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) A distance 5 rotated surface code. Data qubits (•) are on vertices of the grid, while ancilla qubits (•) are on the faces. Each square face measures alternating X and Z type stabilizers. Stabilizers on the boundary include only two data qubits. Circuits for (b) Z and (c) X syndrome extraction on the surface code. The ancilla is prepared in the |0⟩state and measures Z1Z2Z3Z4 or X1X2X3X4 on the four adjacent data qubits di. Vertical dashed lines indicate when crosstalk noise occurs after the entangling CNOT gates in each syndrome extraction circuit.

FIG. 2. Schmidt values squared for a surface code simula- tion with d = 3, 5, 7, 9. The rapid exponential decay in the Schmidt values shown here allows for substantial truncation of the tensor network without impairing its representation of the surface code state. For a code with n physical qubits, the Schmidt values are across the cut between n/2 and n/2 −1.

FIG. 3. The logical error rate plotted for various levels of truncation for a distance d = 9 surface code. Despite doubling the number of terms not truncated at each step, the measured error rates rapidly converge. Note that reducing the number of remaining terms reduces the logical error rate.

FIG. 4. Logical error rates for the surface code under the baseline Pauli noise model with no crosstalk compared with (a) incoherent Pauli twirled crosstalk and (b) coherent crosstalk noise. The Pauli only noise model is the least detrimental to logical error rates, while the coherent crosstalk noise model is the most detrimental. The vertical lines indicate the threshold for each type of noise.

FIG. 6. Logical error rates under each noise model for d = 9. The inclusion of coherent crosstalk noise increases logical error rates over the baseline Pauli noise model, while coherent crosstalk noise in random directions somewhat reduces logical error rates again.
